cards.back: H(s) = Cs/(LCs²+RCs+1) = (s/L)/(s²+(R/L)s+1/(LC)). Input = voltage, output = current.

cards.front: Natural frequency ωₙ of RLC circuit?
cards.back: ωₙ = 1/√(LC). Depends only on L and C, not R. The frequency of undamped oscillation.

cards.front: Damping ratio ζ of series RLC?
cards.back: ζ = (R/2)√(C/L). Proportional to R. More resistance = more damping.
Overdamped: ζ>1 (two real poles, slow). Critically damped: ζ=1 (repeated pole, fastest non-oscillating). Underdamped: ζ<1 (complex poles, oscillating).

cards.front: Total s-domain impedance of series RLC?
cards.back: Z = R + Ls + 1/(Cs). Series impedances add, same as series resistances.
R = 2√(L/C). At this value, ζ=1 and the system returns to equilibrium as fast as possible without overshooting.

cards.front: What if R=0 in an RLC circuit?
cards.back: ζ=0, poles at ±jωₙ. Perfect undamped oscillation at ωₙ=1/√(LC) forever. Ideal LC oscillator.

cards.front: Why does RLC have two poles?
cards.back: Two independent energy storage elements (L and C) → second-order system → two poles. Each storage element adds one to the system order.

cards.front: Derive H(s) for series RLC via impedance.
cards.back: Z=R+Ls+1/(Cs). H=I/V=1/Z=1/(R+Ls+1/Cs)=Cs/(LCs²+RCs+1). Done in one step!

cards.front: How do poles relate to response type?
cards.back: Real distinct poles → overdamped (no oscillation). Repeated real → critically damped. Complex conjugate → underdamped (oscillation at ωd=ωₙ√(1−ζ²)).
RC: 1 pole, no oscillation, H=1/(1+RCs). RLC: 2 poles, can oscillate if underdamped, H=Cs/(LCs²+RCs+1). Adding L makes the system second-order.
